1:
The chapter opens a new register — from the promise of a house in the previous chapter to the wars that secure the kingdom — and the witnesses first fix its scope. John Gill reads the opening 'after this' as a deliberate reminder that David's rest was short-lived and that he 'had other work to do than to build the house of God.' Keil and Delitzsch widen the lens further still: this is not a record of the wars that followed the promise of 2 Samuel 7 only, but a summary survey of David's whole reign, gathering every nation he ever conquered — Philistines, Moabites, the Syrians of Zobah and Damascus, Ammon, Amalek, and Edom — into one panorama of supremacy, the war with Ammon alone being reserved for fuller treatment in 2 Samuel 10.

The one exegetical knot is the obscure phrase Metheg-ammah. The grammatical-historical commentators, reading against the parallel 'David took Gath and her towns' in 1 Chronicles 18:1, take it as a place or a metaphor for one. Charles Ellicott renders it 'the bridle — that is, the key — of the metropolis,' Gath being the chief of the five Philistine cities and long tributary even to Solomon (1 Kings 4:24). John Wesley agrees: Gath stood on the hill of Ammah, and as the one Philistine city with a king it had been 'the bridle which had hitherto kept the Israelites in subjection,' now wrested from their hand. Adam Clarke records the rival reading, the Vulgate's 'David removed the bondage of the tribute,' and confesses the versions differ so widely that no place called Metheg-ammah is otherwise known, so that the plain sense is simply the reversal of Philistine dominion.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the conquest into type. The Philistines had struck when they thought David weak (2 Samuel 5:17) and got the worst of it; now, strong, he masters their country and turns their own curb against them — 'when the strong man is disarmed, the armour wherein he trusted is taken from him' (Luke 11:22). What Samson had only begun (Judges 13:5), David completes, for Israel was still not in full possession of the country God had entitled them to. And here the witnesses land where they will return again and again through the chapter: David's victories foreshadow the Son of David, who 'shall tread them all under his feet and make the saints more than conquerors.'
2:
David turns next to Moab, and the witnesses read the verse for its severity, its cause, and its fulfilment of prophecy. On cause they are agreed: what makes David's harshness striking is that Moab had once sheltered him, for he had entrusted his parents to the king of Moab in his exile (1 Samuel 22:3). John Gill and John Wesley note that the Moabites were nonetheless 'perpetual and sworn enemies' of Israel, forbidden the congregation of the Lord, so that their old enmity reasserted itself. Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch add the Jewish tradition that supplies a motive: the Moabites had proved false to their trust and put David's parents to death — likely enough, since his father and mother are never mentioned again after being left there.

The severity itself provokes the chapter's first real division. Most take the measuring-line literally of the men. Keil reads that David made the Moabites lie on the ground and 'measured two lines to put to death, and one full line to keep alive,' so that two-thirds of the men perished; Ellicott pictures the prostrate captives measured by a cord divided into three, and Wesley and Gill concur that the land was conquered and its people so distributed. Against this stands Adam Clarke, who thinks the text 'will bear a meaning much more reputable to that king': the line was cast over the cities, two-thirds of the strong places being razed while the rest were spared — 'death,' on his reading, referring by metaphor to the towns, not the population.

Beneath the dispute the witnesses find one telling detail of mercy: though two lines were marked for destruction, the portion appointed to live is ordered to be 'one full line' — measured generously, the benefit of the doubt given to life. And all of them hear behind the scene the ancient oracle now coming true, that 'a sceptre shall arise out of Israel, and shall smite the corners of Moab' (Numbers 24:17); Ellicott sees here at least a partial fulfilment of Balaam's word. The chapter's closing phrase, that Moab 'brought gifts,' the commentators take with one voice as a settled euphemism — the Moabites now paid tribute, made servants to David (compare the same idiom in v.6).
3:
The war moves north to Hadadezer of Zobah, and the grammatical-historical commentators spend their labour on two puzzles — where Zobah lay, and whose 'hand' was being recovered at the Euphrates. On the first they are candid about their ignorance. John Gill canvasses the guesses — Benjamin of Tudela's Aleppo, Josephus' Sophene — and notes that Zobah lay so near Israel that the rabbis debated whether it should be reckoned part of the land. Keil and Delitzsch reject both Nisibis and Aleppo as too far off and place Aram-Zobah near Damascus, a kingdom that in 1 Samuel 14:47 stood alongside Moab, Ammon, and Edom among Israel's neighbours.

On the name itself Gill and Charles Ellicott note the ready confusion of Hadadezer and Hadarezer in the Chronicles, the Hebrew letters d and r being nearly identical; Ellicott judges 'Hadadezer' the right form, since Hadad was the chief idol of the Syrians. The second puzzle is the clause 'as he went to recover his border at the river.' The grammatical-historical reading refers the 'hand' to Hadadezer: to 'cause his hand to return' means to re-establish his own power, and Ellicott, following 'to establish his dominion' in 1 Chronicles 18:3, unfolds the whole campaign from 2 Samuel 10:16 — Hadadezer summoning the tribes beyond the Euphrates until David cut short his plans.

Ellicott adds that when or by what means Zobah had come to be united under a single sovereign is unknown, but from this verse taken with 2 Samuel 10:6 it is plain that Hadadezer was 'a monarch of considerable power,' controlling tribes beyond the Euphrates; and Gill records that some of the rabbis reckoned conquered Zobah so near Israel as to be counted, in several respects, equal to the land itself.

The Wesleyan John Wesley and Clarke, however, turn the clause toward David: it was David who 'went to recover his rights and establish his dominion as far as Euphrates,' remembering the grant God made to Abraham's seed of all the land to that river (Genesis 15:18). So the schools divide gently over the pronoun: is the border Hadadezer's, which he marched to restore, or David's, which the son of Jesse marched to claim? Either way the outcome is the same — the Syrian king who reached for the Euphrates is broken by the king of Israel, and the promised boundary of the covenant land, held out to the patriarch centuries before, begins at last to be realized under David's hand.
4:
The tally of the spoil raises the chapter's most persistent difficulty — the numbers — and the witnesses labour to harmonize the account with its parallel in Chronicles. John Gill notes that 'chariots' has dropped from the Hebrew here, which reads only 'a thousand and seven hundred horsemen,' and supplies it from 1 Chronicles 18:4. On the discrepancy of seven hundred horsemen here against seven thousand there, Gill and John Wesley offer the same reconciliation: read 'seven hundred companies of horse,' each company numbering ten under a captain, so that the seven hundred officers commanded seven thousand men. Charles Ellicott reaches the figure more simply, judging that seven hundred should be seven thousand — a fitter proportion to twenty thousand foot — the whole difference lying in 'two dots over the letter' that marks the Hebrew numeral.

The houghing of the horses divides them. Most read it plainly: Keil, Ellicott, and Wesley take David to have 'houghed,' that is hamstrung, the chariot- and cavalry-horses, rendering them useless for war (as Joshua was commanded at the waters of Merom, Joshua 11:6), reserving only enough for a hundred chariots. Adam Clarke dissents on both humanity and grammar: the maiming would be needless cruelty after a complete victory, and, he urges, the verb does not mean 'houghed' at all but that 'David disjointed all the chariots,' destroying the vehicles and keeping a hundred for himself.

What unites them is the theology of the reserved remnant. To destroy chariots was, Clarke says, 'a matter of sound policy and strict piety,' since God had censured those who trust in them; and the Septuagint, he notes, renders the verb his way, that David 'disabled all the chariots' but kept a hundred, likely as baggage-wagons. Ellicott, for his part, allows that David may have reserved the horses for a hundred chariots as 'a means of more rapid communication with the distant parts of his increasing empire,' yet worries that this very reservation was 'the entering wedge' for Solomon's later violation of the law that a king must not multiply horses to himself nor send to Egypt for them (Deuteronomy 17:16).

Behind the arithmetic the point is spiritual, and the witnesses draw it out. David, who wrote that 'a horse is a vain thing for safety' (Psalm 33:17), placed his strength not in cavalry but in the living God, and so he cripples the very engine of the world's confidence rather than turn it to his own trust. The hamstringing of the horses — the same measure God had once commanded Joshua at the waters of Merom (Joshua 11:9) — is thus no mere act of war but a confession: the victory just won was the Lord's, and the king will not now lean on the arm of flesh he has taken from his enemy.
5:
When the Syrians of Damascus march to relieve Hadadezer, they too are broken, and the witnesses, working with thin material, chiefly locate the players and reach outside Scripture for corroboration. John Wesley gives the plainest gloss: 'of Damascus' means those 'who were subject to Damascus, the chief city of Syria' — a distinct branch of the Syrian people drawn into the war on Hadadezer's side. Charles Ellicott agrees that they are named from their capital, and calls them 'the most powerful branch of the Syrian race,' now losing twenty-two thousand men in the field.

What most interests John Gill and Ellicott is the external witness to the event. Both cite the pagan historian Nicolaus of Damascus, preserved by Josephus, who tells of one Adad reigning over Damascus and 'the other Syria without Phoenicia,' who 'made war with David king of Judea and was routed by him at Euphrates.' Gill weighs whether this Adad was Hadadezer himself — the first king of Damascus, joining that city to the kingdom of Zobah, and lending his name to all the kings of Damascus that came after — or, with Josephus, a separate ally who came to Hadadezer's aid. Ellicott is content simply to note that Josephus 'quotes from the historian Nicolaus a mention of the defeat of Hadad at this place by David,' a rare instance of an outside source touching the sacred record.

The verse is brief, but its place in the chapter is not small: it shows the reach of David's victory spreading beyond a single foe. A coalition is forming against Israel, Damascus rising to shore up Zobah, and each fresh confederation only draws more nations into the same defeat. The most powerful branch of the Syrian people commits its full strength — twenty-two thousand men — and loses them all in a day, so that the way lies open for the garrisons of the next verse.

The commentators note, as they will again through the chapter, that the enemies of God's people who league together for their own security find instead their common ruin. And they weigh, too, the significance of the corroboration: that a heathen chronicler, centuries removed and no friend to Israel, should independently record David's rout of the Syrian king only underscores for Gill and Ellicott the historical solidity of a narrative Scripture tells not to magnify David but to trace the hand of God preserving him whithersoever he went.
6:
With Damascus defeated, David plants garrisons and the Syrians become tributary, and the witnesses attend both to the word 'garrisons' and to the refrain that closes the verse. On the word, John Gill reports the divided renderings: he places the garrisons in Coele-Syria, between Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon, whose chief city was Damascus, while the Targum and the rabbis (Ben Gersom, R. Isaiah) read not soldiers but 'commanders' or 'governors' set over the principal cities. Charles Ellicott parses the Hebrew: its root sense is 'something placed, and then placed over,' so that it can mean an officer (as in 1 Kings 4:5) or a garrison (as in 1 Samuel 13:3) — here, he judges, most likely a garrison. Keil and Delitzsch settle it firmly: the word denotes 'not governors or officers, but military posts.'

The Syrians of Damascus, so posted, 'became servants to David, and brought gifts' — the same tribute-idiom the commentators met in the case of Moab. Damascus, which had risen to defend Zobah, is now itself absorbed into the empire, its wealth flowing to Jerusalem and its cities held by Israelite arms. Keil marks how the survey moves outward in widening rings: Philistia, Moab, Zobah, and now Damascus, each conquest consolidated by occupation rather than merely by a battle won.

But it is the closing line that the witnesses press home as the theological heart of the whole chapter: 'And the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went.' This refrain, repeated word for word at v.14, turns the catalogue of victories from a chronicle of David's prowess into a testimony to God's protection. Keil notes that the garrisons and the tribute-idiom 'brought gifts' bind this Syrian conquest to the pattern already seen with Moab in v.2: the beaten nation is not merely defeated but incorporated, held by Israelite posts and paying its yearly due.

The point of the refrain, the commentators agree, is the one David himself made so often in his psalms — that it was not his sword or his policy but the covering hand of God that carried him through the day of battle. The servant conquers only because the Lord goes with him; and so the historian, in the very act of tallying David's triumphs, quietly lifts the credit off the king and lays it on his God. To that Preserver, not to the conqueror, the glory belongs — a lesson the chapter will state twice over before it is done.
7:
David carries off the golden shields of Hadadezer's servants to Jerusalem, and the witnesses divide over just what the golden things were and how they were borne. On the objects, John Gill assembles the ancient versions like a jeweller's tray: Aquila makes them golden chains, the Septuagint golden bracelets, the Syriac (with Jarchi and R. Isaiah) quivers of gold, while Josephus and the received text keep 'shields.' Adam Clarke frankly throws up his hands — 'we know not what these were' — listing arms, quivers, bracelets, collars, and shields, and settling only for 'costly ornaments' by which the Syrian soldiers were decked and distinguished.

Charles Ellicott holds to 'shields of gold' and reads the detail forward: Solomon too 'made shields of gold' as a mark of oriental magnificence (1 Kings 10:17), and both alike were finally carried off when Shishak plundered Jerusalem (1 Kings 14:25). He is careful, though, to flag the Septuagint's error here — a gloss claiming Shishak seized these shields of David, which is impossible, since the shields Shishak took were Solomon's own.

A smaller question concerns the phrase 'that were on the servants of Hadadezer.' Clarke takes 'servants' as the king's choice troops or bodyguard — the Syrian answer to Alexander's silver-shielded argyraspides — who bore the golden shields into battle. John Wesley demurs: the shields were not carried into the field at all but were 'committed to their custody, as being kept in the king's armoury,' so that 'on the servants' means simply in the keeping of the royal attendants, since it is not likely such costly things were risked in the press of the fight.

However the detail is resolved, the witnesses see one movement that matters most: the gold of a heathen king, whether ornament or armour, is stripped from Damascus and brought up to the city of David. And they let the eye run forward to the sequel — for this gold, like Solomon's shields after it, sat in Jerusalem only for a season before the tide of history bore some of it away again; Ellicott's care to correct the Septuagint on Shishak is itself a reminder that earthly treasure changes hands. The spoil David gathers here is destined, as the next verses will show, not for royal display but for the house of God, where alone what is dedicated is 'most comfortably' secure.
8:
From the cities of Hadadezer David takes 'exceeding much brass,' and the witnesses spend their effort on the place-names and, more happily, on what became of the metal. The names first: John Gill, Keil and Delitzsch, and Charles Ellicott all note that 'Betah and Berothai' appear in 1 Chronicles 18:8 as 'Tibhath and Chun' — Tibhath a simple transposition of the letters of Betah — and confess the towns cannot be located with certainty. Gill connects Berothai with the Berothah of Ezekiel 47:16 and Ptolemy's Barathena; Keil suspects a copyist's slip in the Hebrew and leaves the geography open; Wesley simply allows that the two records may name the same cities differently, 'the one by the Hebrews, the other by the Syrians,' or may name four cities in all.

Whatever the map, the destination of the brass is what lifts the verse. Both Keil and Ellicott, following the Chronicler, observe that of this 'exceeding much brass' Solomon afterward 'made the brazen sea, and the pillars, and the vessels' of the temple — the great laver, the twin columns Jachin and Boaz, and the sacred implements all cast from the plunder of Syria. Ellicott adds the metallurgist's caution that the metal was very likely not brass in the modern sense but bronze or copper, quantities of which were anciently carried from Syria into Egypt.

Keil presses the philology a little further, suspecting that the form 'Betah' arose by a copyist's slip from a word for 'slaughter,' and noting that the Septuagint interpolates the notice of the temple furniture here as well, drawing it in from the Chronicler; but the doctrinal weight, he agrees, lies not in the names but in the destination of the metal. The very same 'much brass' reappears in 1 Chronicles 18:8 as the stock from which the sacred vessels were made.

Here the witnesses touch a theme that will crown the next verses: the spoils of war becoming the furniture of worship. David, forbidden to build the house himself, is nonetheless gathering its very materials; the bronze that armed a pagan king against Israel is melted down to stand in the courts of the Lord — the great sea in which the priests would wash, the twin pillars at the porch, the lavers and shovels and basins of the daily service. What was forged for conquest is consecrated for praise — a quiet parable, the commentators sense, of how in God's economy the wealth and strength of the nations are at last turned to the service of his sanctuary.
9:
The scene shifts from battle to diplomacy as Toi of Hamath hears of Hadadezer's defeat, and the witnesses set the stage geographically and politically. John Gill and Adam Clarke identify Hamath as a small Syrian kingdom to the north of Canaan, its capital on the Orontes — Clarke equating it with the famous Emesa — and note that its king (Toi in the Hebrew, Tou in the Chronicles, 'king of Antioch' in the Targum) had been at war with the very Hadadezer David had just crushed. Charles Ellicott traces Hamath's long after-history: tributary to Solomon (2 Chronicles 8:3), later independent, recovered by Jeroboam II (2 Kings 14:28), and at last swallowed by Assyria (2 Kings 19:13) — a city Amos would call 'Hamath the great' (Amos 6:2).

Keil and Delitzsch read the diplomacy exactly: Toi, whose own enemy had now been broken by David, seeks his friendship, sending his son 'to ask him of peace' — to salute and to bless him, that is, to congratulate him on the victory (the same courtesy-idiom as in Genesis 43:27) — and conveying a rich present of vessels of silver, gold, and brass.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the shrewd lesson. Here is 'the court made to David by the king of Hamath,' a neighbouring prince securing and strengthening himself by alliance; and David loses nothing by taking this little king under his protection, for the wealth he had won from his enemies by the sword he now receives from this friend by way of present. 'Better get by composition than by compulsion,' Henry observes — the fear of David's arms and the favour of David's friendship together enlarging his dominion.

So the verse marks a turn in the chapter's tone. Where the earlier nations were beaten into servitude, Hamath comes voluntarily, its king glad that David has broken the power he himself had feared. The victory over Hadadezer thus wins David not only tributaries but an ally; and the present of silver, gold, and brass that Toi's embassy carries will, in David's hands, meet the same fate as the plunder of the conquered — dedicated, as the next verses tell, to the God whose preserving hand had made David worth courting. Even the peace he keeps enriches the kingdom, and beyond the kingdom, the house of God.
10:
The embassy is now named, and the witnesses concentrate on the ambassador's name and the character of his errand. Toi 'sent Joram his son unto king David' — but John Gill and Charles Ellicott at once observe that the parallel calls him Hadoram (1 Chronicles 18:10). The two schools handle the variant differently. Ellicott takes Joram as simply 'the Jewish form of the same name,' a natural adaptation of a Syrian prince's name to Hebrew ears. Keil and Delitzsch are more exacting: Hadoram is the true reading and Joram 'an error of the pen,' since Joram is a distinctly Israelitish, Jehovah-compounded name that one would not expect to find borne by an Aramaean.

On the errand itself the commentators agree. The son is sent 'to salute him, and to bless him' — where 'to bless,' Ellicott notes, is simply to congratulate, the word so rendered in the Chronicles — and to acknowledge David's defeat of the common enemy, 'for Hadadezer had wars with Toi.' Ellicott marks the honour of it: 'an embassy headed by the king's son was an especially honourable one,' and the large presents he carried have, he says, 'something of the character of tribute.'

Keil presses his case with a further reason: not only is Joram a Jehovah-compounded name unlikely on an Aramaean prince, but Hadoram is found among Arabic and Aramaean names elsewhere in the genealogies, whereas the few later bearers of Hadoram or Adoram cannot be shown to be Israelites. Ellicott, less troubled, is content that a Syrian name should be given its natural Hebrew dress in the sacred record, as foreign names so often were.

So the verse quietly registers a change in David's standing among the nations. He is no longer merely a warrior beating back aggressors; he is a sovereign to whose court the neighbouring kingdoms send their princes with gifts and congratulations, and the honour of an embassy led by a king's own son is not lost on the witnesses. The vessels of silver, gold, and brass that Joram brings are freely offered, not wrung from a beaten foe — and yet, as verse eleven will show, David will treat this friendly gift exactly as he treats the spoils of his enemies, turning both alike toward the one end he has set his heart upon: the building of the Lord's house.
11:
The heart of the chapter's piety now appears: David 'dedicated' the treasure to the Lord. John Gill states it plainly — he 'devoted it to sacred uses, particularly to the building of the house of the Lord,' along with the gold and brass taken from Hadadezer. Charles Ellicott notices why the dedication of Toi's gift is singled out: unlike the spoils of the conquered nations, these were the free present of a friend, yet David consecrates them no less; forbidden himself to build the temple, he 'makes every provision possible for its erection.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes this the crown of all David's wars. It is dedication, he says, that makes David's victories 'far to out-shine Alexander's or Caesar's': they sought their own glory, but he aimed at the glory of God, and 'all the precious things he was master of were dedicated things,' designed for the temple. Henry hears in it a gospel omen — that the house of God should be built of the spoils and presents of Gentile nations foreshadows the day when the kings of the earth shall bring their glory and honour into the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24), and the gain of the nations be made 'holiness to the Lord' (Isaiah 23:18; Micah 4:13).

And Henry presses the pattern into the soul. The idols of gold David had burned (2 Samuel 5:21), but the vessels of gold he dedicated; so 'in the conquest of a soul, by the grace of the Son of David, what stands in opposition to God must be destroyed, every lust mortified and crucified, but what may glorify him must be dedicated, and the property of it altered.' The distinction is exact: the false object of worship is put to the fire, while the good gift wrongly held is not destroyed but consecrated, its ownership transferred to its rightful Lord.

The witnesses land, then, not on David's wealth but on David's aim: a king who counts his conquests worth nothing except as they can be laid up for the worship of God, and whose treasure, thus surrendered, becomes 'most comfortably' his own. Gill and Ellicott alike note the poignancy that David, told he may not build the house (2 Samuel 7:13), spends his reign amassing what his son will one day raise — content to gather the stones and the gold of a temple he will never see, so that the labour of his hand and the spoil of his sword serve a glory beyond his own lifetime.
12:
The verse lists the nations whose spoil David consecrated — Syria, Moab, Ammon, the Philistines, Amalek, and Hadadezer of Zobah — and the witnesses read it as both a summary of the empire and a nest of textual questions. John Gill observes that 'Syria' here means Syria of Damascus, distinct from Zobah, and that it is omitted in the Chronicler's parallel. Charles Ellicott takes up the chief crux: where this text reads 'Syria,' 1 Chronicles 18:11 reads 'Edom,' the two words differing in Hebrew only by the easily confused letters d and r. Rather than make one an error for the other, he judges that both nations were in fact conquered and their spoils dedicated, Syria named here because Edom's defeat has not yet been recounted, and Edom named in Chronicles because the Syrian booty had just been mentioned there.

The mention of Amalek draws special comment. Ellicott notes that this is the sole allusion to a war with Amalek after David came to the throne. Saul had 'utterly destroyed' them (1 Samuel 15), and David had struck them at Ziklag (1 Samuel 30); but they were a nation of many tribes, and only one branch could have fallen under Saul, so that other bands might still have proved troublesome and been defeated at other times.

Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to the next verse's difficulty, arguing that words have dropped from the Masoretic text — 'and smote Edom' — the copyist's eye slipping from 'Aram' to the similar 'Edom,' since the Aramaeans were not defeated in the valley of salt at all but at Medeba (1 Chronicles 19:7) and Helam (2 Samuel 10:17). The list itself, the witnesses observe, is arranged not by the order of the campaigns but as a roll of honour — the six great foes whose gold now lay in the treasury of the Lord.

Beneath the philology the witnesses see the same devout pattern held from verse eleven: six nations subdued, and the treasure of them all — Syrian, Moabite, Ammonite, Philistine, Amalekite, and the spoil of Hadadezer — not hoarded, not squandered on royal splendour, but laid up as an offering to the God who gave the victory. Even Amalek, Israel's oldest and most implacable enemy since the wilderness, at last yields its portion to the sanctuary; and the reader who has followed the chapter's refrain understands why the historian troubles to name them all. Each is a monument, not to David's reach, but to the faithfulness of the God who preserved him whithersoever he went.
13:
'David gat him a name' — and the witnesses ask both how he won the fame and where the battle was fought. On the fame, John Gill notes the Jewish tradition that David's renown lay in his humanity in burying the enemy dead, but judges the text means his conquests: 'he made himself a name,' as one might erect a triumphal arch. Adam Clarke adds the Targum's turn — that David 'gathered a name,' collecting troops to recruit an army thinned by battle — and places the valley of salt on a plain 'about a league from the city of Palmyra or Tadmor in the wilderness.'

The location and the enemy are disputed. Where the Hebrew says David smote 'the Syrians' in the valley of salt, the Chronicler and the title of Psalm 60 say 'Edom' (1 Chronicles 18:12), and Charles Ellicott reconstructs the campaign to reconcile them. The war began against Ammon (2 Samuel 10), who hired the Syrians; David crushed the northern coalition near Hamath, then 'returned' south to the valley of salt — the Arabah below the Dead Sea — where the army was chiefly Edomite, though named 'Syrian' here because the whole confederacy went by the name of its most powerful member. Abishai won that first battle of eighteen thousand, Joab a second of twelve thousand (per the psalm's title), and the power of Edom was broken.

Ellicott follows the sequel to its end: once this first battle of eighteen thousand was won, the whole force of Israel was mustered under Joab to overrun Edom and cut off its males (1 Kings 11:15), certain being excepted, so that their descendants remained relentless foes of Israel in after ages. Ellicott and Matthew Henry both see in it the ancient oracle fulfilled — Isaac's word that Esau should serve Jacob and yet in time break the yoke (Genesis 27:40) — for Edom now became David's servants and remained tributary until Joram's day, when they revolted as the prophecy had foretold.

Yet the witnesses are careful, with the psalm David wrote on this very occasion, to lay the honour where it belongs. Henry: 'It is through God that we do valiantly'; the name David got he was careful to transfer to God, so that the arch of his triumph bears, at its keystone, another's glory. And here Ellicott marks the hinge of the chapter: with this last conquest the historian turns from David's wars abroad to the ordering of his kingdom at home, the same list of officers being given again in 2 Samuel 20:23 — as if to say that the fame won in the field is completed only in the justice of the throne.
14:
Edom is garrisoned and made servant, and the verse closes with the chapter's great refrain repeated. On the occupation, John Gill and Adam Clarke agree in substance: David placed companies of soldiers throughout Edom to hold the country 'in awe' and in subjection, Clarke adding that he 'repaired the strong cities' for the purpose, while Gill records the Targum's and Jarchi's gloss that these were governors or officers of David's own nation, appointed to hold the principal cities and to collect the tribute David exacted. Keil and Delitzsch underline the completeness of it: the verse doubles back on itself for emphasis — 'in Edom; throughout all Edom put he garrisons' — and 'all they of Edom became David's servants,' the whole nation reduced, the survey of conquest now finished.

Then, for the second time (compare v.6), comes the sentence that frames the whole: 'And the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went.' The witnesses take this as the deliberate refrain of the passage, bracketing the catalogue of victories at either end so that no reader can mistake David's success for David's achievement. Between the two occurrences lie Zobah, Damascus, Toi's embassy, the dedicated spoil, and now Edom — and both times the sentence falls at the moment of conquest, as if the historian will not let a single triumph stand without its acknowledgment. Every border secured, every king subdued, is set within the keeping of God.

The Wesleyan John Wesley lifts the refrain to its highest register, reading all David's victories as 'typical of the success of the gospel over the kingdom of Satan, in which the Son of David rode forth, conquering and to conquer' (Revelation 6:2), and will reign till he has brought down all opposing rule and power. It is a reading the whole chapter has been inviting, for again and again the commentators have paused to see in David's subdued enemies a figure of the powers of darkness overthrown by great David's greater Son.

So the chapter's roll of subdued nations — Philistine, Moabite, Syrian, Edomite, Amalekite — becomes for the witnesses a shadow of a greater dominion: the preserved king who conquers on every side prefigures the risen King whose kingdom shall have no end, and whose people, kept as David was kept, are more than conquerors through him. The doubled refrain thus does double duty. On the surface it is the plain historian's confession that David's empire was God's gift; beneath it, the witnesses hear the assurance that the same preserving faithfulness attends the church's long warfare, until every enemy is put under the feet of the Lord's Anointed.
15:
The narrative turns from foreign war to domestic government, and the witnesses read David's reign as the pattern of righteous rule. John Gill marks the extent: he 'reigned over all Israel,' not Judah only, over the whole land promised to Abraham and now reaching, through these conquests, to the Euphrates (Genesis 15:18). Matthew Henry presses that David was 'not so engaged in his wars abroad as to neglect the administration of the government at home': his care extended to every part of his dominion, so that all Israel, even the meanest tribes, were safe under his protection and shared the fruits of his good government.

Henry finds two things in 'he executed judgment and justice unto all his people.' First, industry and access — David 'neither did wrong nor denied or delayed right to any,' welcoming even the lowliest to his council-board and giving close application to business. Second, impartiality — he 'never perverted justice through favour or affection, nor had respect of persons in judgment.' Herein, Henry says, David was 'a type of Christ, who was faithful and true, and who doth in righteousness both judge and make war' (Revelation 19:11; compare the prayer for the king in Psalm 72:1).

Henry lingers on the impartiality: David 'never perverted justice through favour or affection, nor had respect of persons in judgment,' but heard even the meanest of the people and the meanest tribes at his council-board. This is the mark, the witnesses agree, that distinguishes a king after God's own heart from a mere conqueror — that the strength which subdued the nations abroad is matched by an even-handed righteousness at home, so that David's throne is established not on force alone but on 'judgment and justice,' the very foundations the psalm assigns to God's own seat.

The witnesses hear in this brief verse the summary and standard of the whole reign: a king strong enough to conquer the nations, and just enough to give every subject his right. The two belong together, for the same hand that the Lord preserved in battle he now guides in judgment. And the type looks forward — the reign of David, righteous and universal, becomes a shadow of the reign of great David's greater Son, whose government executes a perfect judgment and justice, and under whose sceptre all his people, gathered from every tribe and nation, dwell secure. What follows, the roll of David's officers, only fills in the machinery of a kingdom so administered.
16:
The list of David's ministers begins, and the witnesses gloss the two offices named. On Joab, 'over the host,' John Gill recalls that his command was owed not only to kinship — he was David's sister's son — but to David's own promise that whoever first smote the Jebusites should be chief; Joab did so, and so held the generalship. Adam Clarke simply calls him 'general and commander-in-chief over all the army.'

The office of 'recorder' (Hebrew mazkir) draws more comment, and the schools give it three shades. Clarke reads it as 'remembrancer,' a chronicler who 'kept a strict journal of all the proceedings of the king and operations of his army.' John Wesley takes it more narrowly as a treasurer, 'who examined all the accounts, and kept records of them.' Charles Ellicott gives it the widest scope: a distinct office from that of scribe, and one of considerable importance (as later passages show, 2 Kings 18:18; 2 Chronicles 34:8; Esther 6:1), something like the modern chancellor, who not only registered the king's decrees but served as his adviser — the same Jehoshaphat continuing in the post into Solomon's early reign (1 Kings 4:3).

So the witnesses disagree, but only over the compass of the office — annalist, treasurer, or chancellor — not over its dignity. Ellicott is careful to distinguish the recorder (the mazkir) from the scribe or secretary of the next verse, two separate offices too easily confused; the recorder registered the king's decrees and advised him, while the scribe drew up the despatches. That the same Jehoshaphat still held the post in Solomon's early years (1 Kings 4:3) shows the continuity of the administration David founded.

What the verse shows, taken with those around it, is the ordered machinery of a settled kingdom: David, the first king to enjoy an established government (Saul's reign, as the Puritan reading notes, being short and unsettled), modelling an administration with a commander for the army and a recorder at the king's side. The kingdom preserved in battle is now a kingdom furnished with faithful officers, each in his place — the visible form of that judgment and justice the previous verse ascribed to the throne.
17:
The list continues with the priests and the scribe, and here the witnesses meet a genuine genealogical tangle. All agree on the broad picture: John Gill insists Zadok and Ahimelech were not two high priests — there being only one at a time — but the principal priests serving under Abiathar, the one of the line of Eleazar and the other of Ithamar, with Eli's house now in its foretold decline. Keil and Delitzsch refine it: Zadok, of Eleazar's line, officiated at the tabernacle in Gibeon (1 Chronicles 16:39), Ahimelech probably before the ark on Zion.

The knot is the phrase 'Ahimelech the son of Abiathar,' when elsewhere Abiathar is plainly the son of Ahimelech (1 Samuel 22:20), and it is Zadok and Abiathar, not Ahimelech, who appear as David's two priests through the rest of the book. Keil refuses the easy fix of simply transposing the names to read 'Abiathar the son of Ahimelech,' judging that the difficulty cannot be so removed. Charles Ellicott lays the whole perplexity out — Ahimelech the high priest slain by Saul, Abiathar his son who fled to David and was later deposed by Solomon, and yet the Lord's own words placing David's eating of the shewbread 'in the days of Abiathar the high priest' (Mark 2:26) — and offers what he calls the simple solution: that both names were borne alike by father and by son, so that each is sometimes called by the one, sometimes by the other.

The scribe raises no such storm: Adam Clarke and John Wesley agree that Seraiah was the king's private secretary, or 'secretary of state,' who drew up the public despatches. Gill adds the larger movement behind the priestly names — that the house of Eli was now in its long-foretold decline, and the high priesthood, held for a season by Abiathar of Ithamar's line, was on its way to passing to Zadok of Eleazar's, as it would under Solomon; so that even this dry register quietly records a providence at work.

The witnesses, then, do not resolve the priestly puzzle to a single answer — Keil holding it an unsolved difficulty, Ellicott a case of shared names borne by father and son alike — but they agree on what matters for the chapter: David's court was served by a duly ordered priesthood as well as by soldiers and secretaries, worship and government alike set on a settled footing under the preserved king. The two altars, at Gibeon and before the ark on Zion, are each supplied; the sanctuary is as carefully officered as the army, and the reader is meant to see a kingdom in which the service of God is not an afterthought but a chief concern of the throne.
18:
The roll of officers closes with Benaiah over 'the Cherethites and the Pelethites,' and this pair of names sets off the verse's one lively debate. All agree that they were David's bodyguard — John Gill citing Josephus that they were 'the king's bodyguards,' Charles Ellicott calling them 'the most trusted part of the king's army,' and Benaiah of Kabzeel their captain, later promoted by Solomon to be general-in-chief (1 Kings 2:35). But what the two names mean divides the witnesses three ways.

The first reading makes them a tribal or national designation — foreign mercenaries. Ellicott notes a tribe of Cherethites living south of Philistia (1 Samuel 30:14; Ezekiel 25:16; Zephaniah 2:5), and John Wesley allows they may be named 'from some country' — a branch of the Philistines or their neighbours — the objection that David would not employ heathen met by supposing they had embraced Israel's religion. The second reading, which Gill and Adam Clarke think 'by far the most likely,' follows the Targum in translating the names as weapons: 'the archers and the slingers,' David's picked marksmen. The third, urged by Keil and Delitzsch and the Chaldee and Syriac, makes them appellatives of office — 'executioners and runners' — from roots meaning to cut down and to be swift, the royal halberdiers who carried out the king's sentence and the couriers who bore his word.

Gill, weighing all three, leans to native Israelites — the chief men who had been with David in Philistia and at Ziklag — rather than heathen so near the king's person; but he flatly rejects the fanciful rabbinic reading that would make them the Sanhedrin or the Urim and Thummim. The verse ends by noting that 'David's sons were chief rulers,' set, as the Chronicler explains, near the king's own hand (1 Chronicles 18:17). So the chapter that opened with the sweep of David's wars closes with the quiet order of his household — army, bodyguard, priests, scribes, and sons each in place — the preserved king ruling a settled kingdom, its every office a witness that the Lord had established David's throne.
